structured_choices.data_source: Publicly available dermatoscopic image dataset (~10,000 labeled images). Source: HAM10000 or ISIC Archive (specify the exact dataset version and download date). Images span multiple years and acquisition devices. Metadata recorded: image source, acquisition device, image quality indicators (if available), and dermatologist label with inter-rater agreement scores where available.
structured_choices.label_definition: Multi-class diagnostic label assigned by board-certified dermatologists. Class categories: melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion, and other (per ISIC or HAM10000 standard taxonomy). Labels treated as ground truth; however, label uncertainty is acknowledged and controlled for via inter-rater agreement filtering (see confound controls).
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split (not temporal, as the dataset lacks precise temporal ordering suitable for sequential splits, and the deployment context is per-image diagnosis, not time-series prediction). Split: 60% training (6,000 images), 20% validation (2,000 images), 20% test (2,000 images). Stratification is performed on the diagnostic class label to ensure each split preserves the class distribution of the full dataset. This prevents class imbalance from biasing model selection or test evaluation. Rationale: Stratified random split is appropriate here because (1) the task is per-image classification with no inherent temporal or site-based structure, (2) class imbalance exists (melanoma is rarer than nevi) and stratification protects against selection bias, and (3) the deployment scenario is independent diagnosis, not sequential or grouped prediction.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformations (image normalization, augmentation parameter fitting, feature scaling for traditional methods) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. Image normalization (e.g., standardization to zero mean and unit variance) is computed from the training set pixel statistics and applied to validation/test. No data leakage: validation and test statistics are never used during preprocessing fitting.
structured_choices.features: CNN approach: Raw pixel values (uint8, 0–255) from images resized to a standard resolution (224×224, justified by standard practice for transfer learning and computational efficiency). Images are converted to RGB if grayscale. Traditional baseline approach: Handcrafted features extracted from the same resized, normalized images. Feature set includes: (1) color descriptors (mean/std of R, G, B channels; HSV color histogram bins; dominant color), (2) texture descriptors (Haralick features computed from gray-level co-occurrence matrix; Local Binary Pattern histograms; Gabor filter responses at multiple scales and orientations), (3) shape descriptors (lesion area, perimeter, compactness, solidity, eccentricity—computed from binary segmentation masks provided in the dataset or derived via Otsu thresholding). Total traditional feature count: ~80–120 features per image. Both approaches use the same input images; no feature leakage between train/val/test.
structured_choices.model: CNN: ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on the training set. Architecture: remove the final 1000-class classification layer; add a new fully connected layer with 8 outputs (one per diagnostic class) with softmax activation. Fine-tuning strategy: freeze early layers (up to layer3), train only the final residual block and classification head for 50 epochs with learning rate 1e-4. Justification: ResNet-50 is a well-established, interpretable baseline that balances depth and computational cost; pre-training on ImageNet provides useful learned features for natural image statistics; fine-tuning (not training from scratch) is standard practice given the relatively modest dataset size (10,000 images) and reduces overfitting risk.
structured_choices.baseline: Traditional feature extraction + Logistic Regression classifier. Rationale: Logistic regression is a strong, interpretable baseline for multi-class problems (using multinomial loss); it does not introduce unnecessary model complexity and allows the comparison to isolate the contribution of learned CNN features vs. hand-crafted features. Tuning effort for baseline: Logistic Regression hyperparameters (regularization strength C, penalty type L1/L2) are tuned via grid search on the validation set using the same primary metric (see Evaluation Metrics). This ensures the baseline receives equivalent tuning rigor as the CNN. Feature scaling for the traditional baseline is fit on the training set (standardization via StandardScaler) and applied to validation/test. Alternative baseline considered and rejected: Random forest on traditional features would conflate the contribution of feature engineering with model complexity; keeping the baseline simpler (logistic regression) isolates the feature representation advantage.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Macro-averaged F1 score. Justification: In a multi-class, imbalanced setting (melanoma is underrepresented), accuracy alone is misleading—a model that classifies everything as the majority class (nevus) would appear accurate but fail clinically. Macro-averaged F1 (average of per-class F1 scores, unweighted) ensures each diagnostic class is equally important in the evaluation. This aligns with the clinical objective: dermatologists require reliable performance across all lesion types, not just common ones. Melanoma (rarest, most clinically critical) must not be sacrificed for overall accuracy. Macro F1 penalizes false negatives for rare classes, which is appropriate for this deployment context.
secondary_metrics: Weighted F1 score (for comparison, reflects class distribution impact)
Per-class precision and recall (to identify any class-specific failure modes)
Confusion matrix (to understand misclassification patterns, e.g., melanoma vs. atypical nevus confusions)
Balanced accuracy (average of per-class recalls, another class-agnostic metric)
ROC-AUC for melanoma detection specifically (one-vs-rest; clinically critical)
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (2,000 images, 20% of data). Hyperparameter tuning for both CNN and baseline is performed via grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set using macro F1 as the selection metric. The validation set is used only for tuning; it is not part of the training set. Early stopping for the CNN is not applied (to avoid using test-adjacent data for stopping criterion); instead, a fixed epoch count (50) is used. The final test set (2,000 images) is held out and evaluated exactly once, after model and hyperparameter selection is complete.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is completely held out during all training and validation phases. It is accessed exactly once, at the end of the experiment, for final performance reporting. No hyperparameter tuning, model selection, or architecture search occurs on the test set. This ensures the test set provides an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound 1 — Dermatologist expertise and subjective labeling: Images are selected from publicly available datasets (HAM10000, ISIC) where labels have undergone multi-expert review and consensus. For images with available inter-rater agreement scores or uncertainty flags, we stratify to ensure both the CNN and baseline are evaluated on the same set of high-confidence labels (e.g., labels with ≥80% inter-rater agreement). Sensitivity analysis: We also report performance on a subset of lower-confidence labels to assess whether the CNN vs. baseline comparison holds under label uncertainty. Known confound 2 — Image quality and device/lighting variation: The dataset inherently contains this variation (multiple acquisition devices, lighting conditions, skin tones). This is a property of the real deployment scenario and is not removed. However, we perform stratified analysis by image source or device type (if metadata is available) to check whether the CNN advantage holds across device types, ensuring the comparison is not driven by one particular device or imaging protocol. A secondary analysis reports accuracy separately for images from different acquisition devices; the CNN should outperform the baseline across all devices. Class imbalance control: Stratified splits ensure all sets have the same class distribution. During training, the CNN and baseline both employ class weighting or weighted sampling to prevent the model from favoring the majority class. Both receive the same class-balancing treatment to ensure fair comparison.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN vs. Traditional Feature Extraction for Dermatoscopic Image Classification

### 1. Data Strategy

We source images from a publicly available, expertly-curated dermatoscopic database (HAM10000 or ISIC Archive, version specified). The dataset contains approximately 10,000 labeled images spanning multiple years, acquisition devices, and lighting conditions. Each image is labeled with one of eight diagnostic classes (melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion, other) by board-certified dermatologists. Labels are treated as ground truth; we filter out images with low inter-rater agreement (below 80%) to reduce label noise and preserve comparison validity. Image metadata (source device, quality flags) are retained for stratified analysis.

**Data quality considerations:** Variations in image quality, device settings, and lighting are inherent to the real-world deployment context and are preserved in the dataset. We do not artificially homogenize images; instead, we use these variations as part of the test-bed to evaluate robustness. A secondary analysis stratifies results by image source to confirm the CNN advantage generalizes across devices.

### 2. Split Strategy

We employ stratified random splitting to ensure class balance is preserved across all three sets. The split is: 60% training (6,000 images), 20% validation (2,000 images), 20% test (2,000 images). Stratification is performed on the diagnostic class label to protect against class imbalance bias—melanoma, for example, is rarer than nevi, and random splitting could accidentally create a test set skewed toward common classes, inflating apparent accuracy.

**Justification:** A temporal or site-based split is inappropriate here because (1) the dataset lacks a clear temporal ordering (images span years but are not sequentially collected in a manner meaningful for time-series structure), and (2) the deployment scenario is per-image diagnosis, not longitudinal or multi-site prediction. A stratified random split preserves the real-world class distribution and ensures both the CNN and baseline are evaluated on identical class proportions, isolating the feature-representation advantage rather than confounding it with class balance differences.

**Leakage prevention:** No information from the validation or test sets is used during training or preprocessing. All preprocessing statistics (pixel normalization parameters, feature scaling coefficients) are computed from the training set only and then applied to validation and test.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**CNN approach:** Images are resized to 224×224 pixels (standard for transfer learning), converted to RGB if necessary, and normalized to zero mean and unit variance using statistics computed from the training set. No data augmentation is applied during evaluation (validation/test use raw normalized images); augmentation is only applied to the training set if desired during fine-tuning.

**Traditional baseline approach:** The same normalized images are used. Handcrafted features are extracted: color descriptors (mean/std of R, G, B; HSV histogram), texture descriptors (Haralick features from co-occurrence matrices; Local Binary Pattern histograms; Gabor filter responses at multiple scales), and shape descriptors (area, perimeter, compactness, solidity, eccentricity from binary segmentation). This yields ~80–120 features per image. Feature scaling (standardization) is fit on the training set and applied to validation/test.

**Feature leakage check:** No feature encodes the label or uses information from validation/test. Segmentation masks (if required for shape features) are provided in the dataset and are not data-leaking.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**CNN model:** ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet. We remove the final 1000-class layer and replace it with a fully connected layer with 8 outputs (one per diagnostic class) and softmax activation. We fine-tune by freezing early layers and training only the final residual block and classification head for 50 epochs with learning rate 1e-4. This approach leverages pre-trained, generalizable features while adapting to the dermoscopy task. It balances performance and computational cost.

**Baseline:** Logistic Regression (multinomial) trained on the handcrafted features. Hyperparameters (regularization strength C, penalty type L1/L2) are tuned via grid search on the validation set. This baseline is chosen because it is simple, interpretable, and allows us to isolate the contribution of learned CNN features; a more complex baseline (e.g., random forest) would conflate the contribution of feature engineering with model complexity.

**Tuning parity:** Both the CNN and baseline receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort via validation-set search. This ensures the comparison is fair and attributes differences to feature representation, not optimization disparity.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Macro-averaged F1 score. In multi-class, imbalanced settings, accuracy is misleading (a classifier that predicts only the majority class appears accurate). Macro F1 averages per-class F1 scores equally, ensuring melanoma detection (rarest, most critical) is weighted equally with common classes. This aligns with the clinical objective: reliable diagnosis across all lesion types.

**Secondary metrics:** Weighted F1, per-class precision/recall, confusion matrix, balanced accuracy, and melanoma-specific ROC-AUC. These reveal whether the CNN advantage is uniform across classes or driven by particular diagnostic categories.

### 6. Validation Approach

The validation set (2,000 images) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection using macro F1 as the criterion. The test set (2,000 images) is completely held out and accessed exactly once, at the end of the experiment, to report final generalization performance. No hyperparameter tuning, model architecture search, or early stopping occurs on the test set.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

**Label uncertainty:** We filter to high inter-rater agreement (≥80%) to reduce label noise. A sensitivity analysis reports CNN vs. baseline performance on lower-confidence labels to confirm the finding is robust.

**Image quality and device variation:** These variations are preserved as part of the real-world scenario. We stratify secondary analysis by image source/device to verify the CNN advantage holds across acquisition protocols.

**Class imbalance:** Both models employ class weighting during training to balance gradient contributions. Stratified splitting ensures validation and test preserve the original class distribution.

**Scope:** This design establishes that CNNs outperform traditional feature extraction on this dataset and metric. It does not establish generalization to entirely different dermatoscopic databases or non-dermatoscopic domains; external validation would be required for that claim.
